12: Gallio (Lucius Junius Annaeus Gallio Novatus), proconsul of Achaia, is one of the most important chronological anchors for NT chronology. A Delphi inscription (SIG3 801) records Gallio as proconsul of Achaia during the 26th acclamation of Claudius, datable to 51-52 CE. Paul appeared before Gallio ca. 51-52 CE; working backward from this gives Paul's Corinthian stay (18 months, Acts 18:11) as ca. 50-52 CE, and his Damascus road conversion ca. 33-34 CE. Gallio's refusal to adjudicate Jewish theological disputes ('if it were a matter of wrongdoing or vicious crime I would have reason to accept your complaint', Acts 18:14) established a legal precedent: Christianity is an internal Jewish dispute, not a new illegal religion.
1: After Athens Paul came to Corinth: Corinth was the capital of the Roman province of Achaia and one of the wealthiest cities in the Roman world. Destroyed by Rome in 146 BCE and refounded as a Roman colony by Julius Caesar in 44 BCE, it was a boom town by Paul's visit (ca. 50-52 CE). Its two harbors — Cenchreae (eastern, Aegean) and Lechaion (western, Adriatic) — made it the trans-shipping hub of Greece, attracting a transient, cosmopolitan population with a reputation for commercial vice. Paul's longest urban mission (18 months) took root here.
2: Aquila, a Jew from Pontus (Black Sea region), and his wife Priscilla had recently come from Italy because Claudius had expelled all Jews from Rome: the Claudius expulsion is confirmed by Suetonius (Claudius 25.4 — 'since the Jews constantly made disturbances at the instigation of Chrestus, he expelled them'). Most scholars date this to 49 CE (Orosius places it in year 9 of Claudius). 'Chrestus' is likely a misspelling of 'Christus' — the disturbance was probably disputes about Jesus in the Roman synagogues. Aquila and Priscilla were thus early Jewish-Christian expellees who carried the faith westward before Paul arrived.
3: Paul stayed with them and worked as a tentmaker (skenopoios): tentmaking (or more broadly, leatherworking/canvas-working) was a skilled craft that provided income in any major city. Paul's self-support policy in Corinth was deliberate (1 Corinthians 9:12-18) — it distinguished his proclamation from that of fee-taking sophists and wonder-workers who were common in cities like Corinth. Working with his hands was both economically necessary and a theological statement about the character of apostolic ministry.
4: He reasoned in the synagogue every Sabbath (dielegeto en tē synagōgē kata pan sabbaton) and persuaded Jews and Greeks: the Corinthian synagogue was the initial mission venue, as in every city. The 'Greeks' (Hellēnas) in the synagogue are the God-fearers — Gentiles attracted to Jewish monotheism and ethics who attended synagogue without formal conversion. This audience of semi-committed Gentiles was Paul's most responsive demographic: they had the OT background to understand his argument without requiring full proselyte commitment.
5: When Silas and Timothy arrived from Macedonia, Paul was occupied with the word (syneicheto tō logō) and testified to the Jews that Jesus was the Christ: the arrival of Silas and Timothy (from Thessalonica and Beroea) with a financial gift (Philippians 4:15; 2 Corinthians 11:9) freed Paul from tentmaking to devote full time to proclamation. The 'constraint by the word' (syneicheto) — pressed, compelled — reflects Paul's own vocabulary for apostolic compulsion (1 Corinthians 9:16: 'woe to me if I do not preach the gospel').
6: When they opposed and reviled him, he shook out his garments and said, 'Your blood be on your own heads' (to haima hymōn epi tēn kephalēn hymōn): the gesture — shaking dust from garments — was a Jewish sign of repudiation (cf. Acts 13:51 — shaking dust from feet). The blood-guilt formula echoes Ezekiel 33:4-9 (the watchman absolved when he warns and is ignored) and Matthew 27:25 (the crowd accepting blood-guilt). Paul declares he has fulfilled his prophetic responsibility to Israel; their rejection transfers moral accountability to them.
7: He left there and went to the house of a man named Titius Justus, a worshipper of God, whose house was next door to the synagogue (sunomorousan tē synagōgē): the relocation next door is tactical — maximum proximity to the rejected audience. Titius Justus is a Roman name suggesting a freedman or Roman citizen; as a God-fearer, he bridges the Jewish and Gentile worlds. The house-church next to the synagogue creates a social geography of mission: those passing the synagogue see an alternative gathering space for the same God's worship.
8: Crispus, the synagogue ruler, believed in the Lord with all his household (episteusen tō kyriō syn holō tō oikō autou): the conversion of the synagogue leader was both a theological victory and a social disruption — the institutional head of Paul's rejected audience now joins the rival house-church. Paul baptized Crispus personally (1 Corinthians 1:14); the household conversion reflects the unit of social solidarity in antiquity — the household converted together under the paterfamilias's decision.
9: 'Do not be afraid, but go on speaking and do not be silent' (mē phobou, alla lalei kai mē siōpēsēs): the divine vision during the night reassures Paul at the precise moment when the synagogue rejection might have prompted withdrawal. The threefold command — don't fear, speak, don't be silent — addresses the specific temptation to withdraw from a hostile environment. The divine promise 'for I am with you' echoes the patriarchal and prophetic reassurances (Genesis 26:24; Jeremiah 1:8; Isaiah 41:10) of divine presence amid opposition.
10: 'No one will attack you to harm you, for I have many in this city who are my people' (laos esti moi polys en tē polei tautē): the divine assurance of a large hidden constituency waiting to receive the gospel explains why Paul should stay. The phrase 'my people' (laos) is the LXX covenant-people term; God declares that his election-purposes for Corinth include many not yet in the visible community. Paul's 18-month ministry and the resulting community (with extensive correspondence in 1-2 Corinthians) fulfills the divine vision.
11: He stayed a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them: eighteen months is Paul's longest recorded stay in any city except Ephesus (three years, Acts 20:31). The Corinthian stay produced the community addressed in 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, and mentioned in Romans. The founding period established the community's character: intellectually diverse, socially stratified (1 Corinthians 1:26-29 implies some wealthy, most not), and doctrinally unstable — requiring the extensive subsequent correspondence.
13: The accusation before Gallio — 'this man is persuading people to worship God contrary to the law' (para ton nomon anapeitlhei tous anthrōpous sebesthia ton theon) — frames the complaint as a violation of Roman legal categories. Under Roman law, Jews had recognized legal status (religio licita); a new movement allegedly departing from authorized Judaism would lack this protection. The accusers are trying to get Roman authority to enforce their community boundaries against Paul by characterizing his teaching as illegal religion.
14: When Paul was about to speak, Gallio said: the proconsul's intervention before Paul can defend himself is legally significant. Gallio cuts off the speech because he has already determined the legal category of the complaint. His ruling — 'if it were a matter of wrongdoing or vicious crime' — establishes the threshold for Roman judicial intervention: crimes against persons or property. Theological disputes within Judaism do not meet this threshold.
15: 'Since it is a matter of questions about words and names and your own law, see to it yourselves — I refuse to be a judge of these things' (kritēs egō toutōn ou boulomai einai): Gallio's ruling establishes an important precedent for the Pauline mission: Roman authority declines jurisdiction over Jewish-Christian theological disputes. This is the same ruling pattern that Festus and Agrippa will apply to Paul in Acts 25:18-19 and Acts 26:31-32. Luke's repeated documentation of Roman non-intervention is a sustained argument for the Christianity's legal legitimacy.
16: He drove them from the tribunal (apelasen autous apo tou bēmatos): Gallio's physical dismissal — expelling the accusers from the judgment platform — is a humiliation for the synagogue leadership. The bēma (judgment seat) in the Corinthian agora has been archaeologically identified; a limestone platform in the center of the city's civic space served as the governor's public court. Gallio's decision is made in the most public possible setting.
17: Then they all seized Sosthenes, the synagogue ruler, and beat him in front of the tribunal, and Gallio paid no attention (ouden toutōn tō Galliōni emelen): Sosthenes's beating is variously interpreted — either the crowd venting frustration at the failed prosecution (Greek mob anti-Semitism), or Jews beating Sosthenes as punishment for the failed case, or believers taking revenge. A Sosthenes appears in 1 Cor 1:1 as Paul's co-author — possibly this same figure, later converted. Gallio's indifference is the pointed end of the judicial narrative: Roman authority neither helps nor hinders the mission.
18: Paul stayed many more days in Corinth, then took leave of the brothers and sailed for Syria with Priscilla and Aquila. At Cenchreae he had cut his hair (keiramenos... tēn kephalēn) for he was under a vow (eucHēn): the vow-completion at Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern harbor) connects Paul's Corinthian ministry to a Nazirite-type vow (Num 6). Paul's maintenance of Jewish religious practices alongside his Gentile-inclusion theology reflects his 'to the Jew I became as a Jew' flexibility (1 Corinthians 9:20). Priscilla and Aquila's inclusion in the travel party begins their Ephesian chapter.
19: They came to Ephesus, and he left Priscilla and Aquila there, while he himself entered the synagogue and reasoned with the Jews (dielegeto tois Ioudaiois): Ephesus was the provincial capital of Asia and Paul's eventual three-year base (Acts 20:31). The brief visit on this occasion is a scouting and community-planting stop — Priscilla and Aquila remain to establish the community that Paul will later return to develop. Their presence in Ephesus (1 Corinthians 16:19) is the foundation Paul builds on in Acts 19:1-41.
20: When they asked him to stay longer (erotōntōn autōn epi pleiDna chronon meinai), he declined: the synagogue's positive response to Paul's initial teaching contrasts with the hostile reception in Corinth and other cities. The Ephesian synagogue invitation to remain is a rare positive signal; Paul's refusal is explained by the commitment in Acts 18:21. The Ephesian community's receptiveness anticipates the extended ministry of chapter 19.
21: 'I must by all means keep the coming feast at Jerusalem' (dei me pantōs tēn eortēn tēn erchomenēn poiēsai eis Hierosolyma): the festival obligation (likely Passover or Pentecost) reflects Paul's continued participation in Jewish pilgrimage practice. His return to the Jerusalem-Antioch home base between journeys (the end of the second journey, beginning of the third) follows the same return-arc pattern as the first journey (Acts 14:26-28). Jerusalem is both the theological center and the source of apostolic accountability.
22: When he had landed at Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church (anabas kai aspAsamenos tēn ekklēsian) and then went down to Antioch: the compressed summary of the Jerusalem visit (implied by 'went up' — the standard term for ascending to Jerusalem) and Antioch return marks the end of the second journey. The formal greeting of the Jerusalem church maintains the apostolic relationship; the Antioch base provides the launch platform for the third journey. Luke's rapid summary keeps the narrative pace without dwelling on inter-journey transitions.
23: After spending some time in Antioch, Paul went through the region of Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples (epistērizōn pantas tous mathētas): the third journey begins with the same pastoral re-visitation pattern as the second (Acts 15:41). The Galatian-Phrygian route covers the first-journey communities (Derbe, Lystra, Iconium, Pisidian Antioch) plus the second-journey additions (Philippi, Thessalonica, Beroea were on the second journey; the third journey apparently revisits Asia Minor first). The 'strengthening' vocabulary is Paul's consistent return-visit priority.
24: A Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, came to Ephesus. He was an eloquent man (anēr logios), competent in the Scriptures: Apollos represents the Alexandrian Jewish intellectual tradition — the same milieu that produced Philo's biblical philosophy. Logios (eloquent, learned) is the vocabulary of high Hellenistic education; Apollos was an educated rhetor as well as a scriptural exegete. Alexandria had the largest Jewish population in the diaspora and an intense tradition of Greek-Jewish intellectual synthesis. Apollos brings this tradition into the church.
25: He had been instructed in the way of the Lord (kateEchēmenos tēn hodon tou kyriou), and being fervent in spirit he spoke and taught accurately the things concerning Jesus, though he knew only the baptism of John: Apollos's instruction 'in the way of the Lord' was apparently complete enough for accurate teaching about Jesus but incomplete regarding the Spirit-baptism. He represents the overlap zone between the Baptist movement and the church — those who knew Jesus's teaching (possibly from witnesses in Alexandria) but had not received the post-Pentecost Spirit. His fervent spirit is authentic even while his understanding is partial.
26: Priscilla and Aquila heard him and took him aside (proselabonto auton) and explained to him the way of God more accurately (akribesterDon autō exetheInto tēn tou theou hodon): the marital pair's role in completing Apollos's theological education is a significant detail — a woman (Priscilla named first, suggesting her prominence) provides doctrinal instruction to a gifted male teacher. The private, non-public correction respects Apollos's dignity while addressing the gap. The model is pastoral wisdom: supplement rather than publicly correct.
27: When he wished to cross to Achaia (dielthein eis tēn Achaian), the brothers encouraged him and wrote to the disciples to welcome him (apodexasthai auton): the letter of commendation from the Ephesian church to Corinth (cheirographia) is the standard Hellenistic practice for introducing trusted travelers to new communities. Paul refers to letters of recommendation in 2 Corinthians 3:1 — the practice was universal. Apollos's reception in Corinth and his powerful refutation of the Jews there (Acts 18:28) established him as a major figure alongside Peter and Paul (1 Corinthians 1:12; Acts 3:4-6).
28: He powerfully refuted the Jews in public (diakatalenkhomenos tous Ioudaious dēmosia), demonstrating by the Scriptures that the Christ was Jesus: Apollos's method is scriptural demonstration — epideiknys dia tōn graphōn (showing through the Scriptures). His Alexandrian background would have given him mastery of both the Greek OT and the allegorical-typological methods of interpretation. His public synagogue debates represent the same pattern as Paul's but with the specific rhetorical skills of the Alexandrian tradition added to the apostolic proclamation.
